Sonnet

When twilight spills her ink on fading day,
And weary suns withdraw their golden claim,
The restless mind begins its quiet play,
Unbound by time, unshackled from its frame.

For in the hush where mortal sounds grow thin,
There stirs a truth too subtle to be named,
A trembling pulse beneath the mortal skin,
By fleeting thoughts and fragile dreams inflamed.

What art is this, that builds from shadowed air
A world more vast than daylight dares to show?
Where loss finds voice and grief is rendered fair,
And seeds of doubt in secret gardens grow.

Yet when the dawn returns with sharpened sight,
We trade that depth for comfort born of light.
